objects.name: CyberNetSec.io
objects.description: CyberNetSec.io is a cybersecurity intelligence platform providing timely, accurate, deduplicated, and actionable threat intelligence for security professionals. The platform aggregates, deduplicates, and enriches threat data from diverse public sources, mapping all threats to MITRE ATT&CK and D3FEND frameworks with operationally-focused analysis, detection guidance, and mitigation recommendations. Operated by security professionals, for the security community.

objects.name: Jason Gomes
objects.description: Cybersecurity practitioner and founder of CyberNetSec.io with over 10 years of specialized experience in security operations, threat intelligence, incident response, and security automation. Expertise spans SOAR/XSOAR orchestration, threat intelligence platforms, SIEM/UEBA analytics, and cyber fusion centers.

objects.name: MuddyWater
objects.description: An Iranian state-sponsored APT group identified as the actor behind a novel espionage campaign starting in early 2026. The group leverages Microsoft Teams for social engineering and masquerades as a ransomware attack to exfiltrate data and establish persistence on networks of strategic interest to Iran.
goals: Cyber espionage and long-term data exfiltration from organizations of strategic value to Iran.

objects.name: Chaos ransomware group
objects.description: A ransomware group identity that MuddyWater masqueraded as in a false flag operation to mislead incident responders and obscure the state-sponsored nature of their espionage campaign.

objects.name: Spearphishing via Service
objects.description: Attackers used Microsoft Teams as the delivery vehicle for a social engineering attack, persuading targets to join a meeting to harvest credentials.

objects.name: Credentials In Files
objects.description: During a Microsoft Teams screen-sharing session, attackers manipulated the victim into typing credentials into a text file, allowing for their capture.

objects.name: Remote Access Software
objects.description: The MuddyWater group deployed legitimate remote management and monitoring (RMM) tools, specifically DWAgent and AnyDesk, to maintain persistent access and evade detection.

objects.name: Masquerading
objects.description: The espionage operation mimicked the tactics of the Chaos ransomware group as a false flag to misdirect forensic analysis towards a criminal, rather than state-sponsored, origin.

objects.name: Web Protocols
objects.description: The deployed remote access trojans and RMM tools communicated with command and control infrastructure over standard web protocols to blend in with normal traffic.

objects.name: PowerShell
objects.description: Used to execute malicious scripts with a bypassed execution policy, as suggested by hunting hints for 'powershell.exe -ExecutionPolicy Bypass'.

objects.name: Game.exe
objects.description: A custom remote access trojan deployed by MuddyWater after gaining initial access.

objects.name: ms_upd.exe
objects.description: A malicious executable signed with a code-signing certificate issued to 'Donald Gay', which has been previously linked to MuddyWater operations.

objects.name: CastleRAT
objects.description: An off-the-shelf malware from the cybercrime ecosystem used by MuddyWater to obscure attribution.

objects.name: Tsundere
objects.description: An off-the-shelf malware from the cybercrime ecosystem used by MuddyWater to blur the lines between their operations and those of financially motivated criminals.

objects.name: Chaos
objects.description: A ransomware family whose group identity was impersonated by MuddyWater as part of a false flag operation.

objects.name: DWAgent
objects.description: A legitimate remote management and monitoring (RMM) tool deployed by MuddyWater to maintain a persistent foothold on compromised networks.

objects.name: AnyDesk
objects.description: A legitimate remote access software used by MuddyWater post-compromise for persistence and data exfiltration.

objects.name: Microsoft Teams
objects.description: Used as the platform for the social engineering attack, where attackers engaged victims in interactive screen-sharing sessions to harvest credentials.

objects.name: MuddyWater Teams Espionage Campaign 2026
objects.description: An espionage campaign beginning in early 2026 where MuddyWater used Microsoft Teams for social engineering and masqueraded as a ransomware group to exfiltrate data from strategic targets.

objects.name: Organizations of strategic interest to Iran
MuddyWater C2 Server
objects.description: Command and Control server used in the 2026 espionage campaign, identified by IP 172.86.126.208.

objects.name: MuddyWater Attack VPS
objects.description: A RouterHosting VPS used as part of the attack infrastructure, identified by IP 172.86.76.127.

objects.name: MuddyWater C2 Network IOCs 2026
objects.description: Detects network traffic to command and control servers used by MuddyWater in their 2026 espionage campaign.
objects.x_netsecio_sigma_rule:
title: MuddyWater C2 Network Connection 2026
id: 9c5b1d4a-8f3e-4b2c-9d6a-1e2f3a4b5c6d
status: experimental
description: Detects outbound network connections to known MuddyWater C2 infrastructure observed in a 2026 espionage campaign.
references:
  - https://www.thehackernews.com/2026/05/muddywater-uses-microsoft-teams-to.html
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-05-07
tags:
  - attack.t1071.001
  - attack.g0069
level: high
logsource:
  category: network_connection
  product: windows
detection:
  selection:
    DestinationIp:
      - '172.86.126.208'
      - '172.86.76.127'
    Initiated: true
  condition: selection

objects.name: MuddyWater Malware Filename IOCs 2026
objects.description: Detects malicious executables used by MuddyWater based on their filenames.
objects.x_netsecio_sigma_rule:
title: MuddyWater Malware Execution by Filename 2026
id: a1b2c3d4-e5f6-4a7b-8c9d-0e1f2a3b4c5d
status: experimental
description: Detects the execution of malicious files by name, as used by MuddyWater in a 2026 espionage campaign.
references:
  - https://www.thehackernews.com/2026/05/muddywater-uses-microsoft-teams-to.html
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-05-07
tags:
  - attack.g0069
level: high
logsource:
  category: process_creation
  product: windows
detection:
  selection:
    Image|endswith:
      - '\ms_upd.exe'
      - '\Game.exe'
  condition: selection

objects.name: Suspicious RMM Tool Execution (DWAgent, AnyDesk)
objects.description: Detects the execution of DWAgent (DWAService.exe) or AnyDesk.exe, which could indicate abuse by MuddyWater for persistence.

objects.name: Suspicious RMM Tool Execution (DWAgent, AnyDesk)
objects.description: Detects the execution of DWAgent (DWAService.exe) or AnyDesk.exe, which could indicate abuse by MuddyWater for persistence.
objects.pattern:
title: Suspicious RMM Tool Execution
id: f8e7d6c5-b4a3-4f2e-1d0c-9b8a7f6e5d4c
status: experimental
description: Detects the execution of DWAgent or AnyDesk, which were used by MuddyWater for persistence. This may generate false positives in environments where these tools are used legitimately.
references:
  - https://www.thehackernews.com/2026/05/muddywater-uses-microsoft-teams-to.html
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-05-07
tags:
  - attack.t1219
  - attack.g0069
level: medium
logsource:
  category: process_creation
  product: windows
detection:
  selection:
    Image|endswith:
      - '\DWAService.exe'
      - '\AnyDesk.exe'
  filter_legit_parent:
    ParentImage|endswith:
      - '\explorer.exe'
      - '\services.exe'
  condition: selection and not filter_legit_parent

objects.name: Suspicious PowerShell Execution Policy Bypass
objects.description: Detects PowerShell being launched with the ExecutionPolicy Bypass flag, a common technique for running untrusted scripts.
objects.pattern: [ipv4-addr:value = '172.86.126.208' OR ipv4-addr:value = '172.86.76.127'] OR [file:name = 'ms_upd.exe' OR file:name = 'Game.exe' OR file:name = 'DWAService.exe']

objects.name: Suspicious PowerShell Execution Policy Bypass
objects.description: Detects PowerShell being launched with the ExecutionPolicy Bypass flag, a common technique for running untrusted scripts.
objects.pattern:
title: Suspicious PowerShell Execution Policy Bypass
id: c9b8a7f6-e5d4-4c3b-2a1f-0e9d8c7b6a5b
status: experimental
description: Detects the use of 'powershell.exe -ExecutionPolicy Bypass', a technique often used to load malicious scripts, as noted in the MuddyWater campaign analysis.
references:
  - https://www.thehackernews.com/2026/05/muddywater-uses-microsoft-teams-to.html
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-05-07
tags:
  - attack.t1059.001
  - attack.g0069
level: medium
logsource:
  category: process_creation
  product: windows
detection:
  selection:
    Image|endswith: '\powershell.exe'
    CommandLine|contains: ' -ExecutionPolicy Bypass'
  condition: selection

objects.name: Iranian APT MuddyWater Masquerades as Ransomware Group in Microsoft Teams-Based Espionage Campaign
objects.description:
## Executive Summary

The Iranian state-sponsored threat group **[MuddyWater](httpshttps://attack.mitre.org/groups/G0069/)** (also known as Mango Sandstorm, Seedworm, and Static Kitten) has been identified as the actor behind a novel espionage campaign that leverages **[Microsoft Teams](https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/microsoft-teams/group-chat-software)** for social engineering and masquerades as a ransomware attack. The operation, which began in early 2026, targets organizations of strategic value to Iran. Attackers engage victims in interactive screen-sharing sessions to harvest credentials, bypass multi-factor authentication (MFA), and install remote access tools for data exfiltration and long-term persistence. This false flag operation, which mimics the **Chaos** ransomware group, is a deliberate tactic to mislead incident responders and delay attribution, highlighting a strategic shift towards blending state-sponsored espionage with common cybercrime techniques.

---

## Threat Overview

The campaign is characterized by its high-touch social engineering vector. Threat actors initiate contact and persuade targets to join a **[Microsoft Teams](https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/microsoft-teams/group-chat-software)** meeting. During the session, the victim is manipulated into running commands or entering credentials into a text file under the guise of a troubleshooting or collaborative exercise. This allows the attackers to capture credentials in real-time, effectively neutralizing MFA protections.

Unlike a typical ransomware attack, the primary objective is not financial extortion through data encryption. Instead, **[MuddyWater](https://attack.mitre.org/groups/G0069/)** focuses on cyber espionage. After gaining initial access, the group deploys legitimate remote management and monitoring (RMM) tools, including `DWAgent` and `AnyDesk`, to maintain a persistent foothold on the compromised network. The use of these dual-use tools allows their malicious activity to blend in with normal administrative traffic.

The targets include government entities and other organizations of strategic interest in the United States, Western countries, the Asia-Pacific (APAC) region, and the Middle East. The use of a false ransomware persona is a calculated deception designed to create confusion and misdirect forensic analysis towards a criminal, rather than state-sponsored, origin.

## Technical Analysis

Attribution to **[MuddyWater](https://attack.mitre.org/groups/G0069/)** was established with "moderate confidence" based on several key technical indicators. A malicious executable, `ms_upd.exe`, was signed with a code-signing certificate issued to "Donald Gay." This specific certificate has been previously linked to **[MuddyWater](https://attack.mitre.org/groups/G0069/)** operations. The group's TTPs in this campaign include:

- **Initial Access:** [`T1566.004 - Spearphishing via Service`](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1566/004/): Attackers use **[Microsoft Teams](https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/microsoft-teams/group-chat-software)** as the delivery vehicle for their social engineering attack.
- **Execution:** The victim is tricked into executing commands or scripts provided by the attacker during the Teams session.
- **Credential Access:** [`T1552.001 - Credentials In Files`](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1552/001/): Attackers convince the user to type credentials into a text file, which they can then access.
- **Defense Evasion & Persistence:** [`T1219 - Remote Access Software`](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1219/): The group deploys legitimate tools like `DWAgent` and `AnyDesk` to maintain access and evade detection. They also used a custom remote access trojan named `Game.exe`.
- **Masquerading:** [`T1036.003 - Rename System Utilities`](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1036/003/): The operation mimics the **Chaos** ransomware group, a classic false flag tactic.
- **Command and Control:** [`T1071.001 - Web Protocols`](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1071/001/): The deployed RATs and RMM tools communicate with C2 infrastructure over standard web protocols.

The attackers also utilized off-the-shelf malware from the cybercrime ecosystem, including `CastleRAT` and `Tsundere`, further blurring the lines between their operations and those of financially motivated criminals.

## Impact Assessment

The primary impact of this campaign is not business disruption from encryption but rather the long-term strategic risk associated with data exfiltration and persistent espionage. For affected organizations, the compromise could lead to the theft of sensitive government secrets, intellectual property, and confidential business information. The establishment of a persistent backdoor creates an ongoing threat, allowing **[MuddyWater](https://attack.mitre.org/groups/G0069/)** to conduct further reconnaissance, move laterally within the network, and exfiltrate data over an extended period. The false flag nature of the attack complicates incident response, potentially leading to incorrect remediation strategies focused on ransomware recovery rather than APT eviction.

## IOCs — Directly from Articles

| Type | Value | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `ip_address_v4` | `172.86.126.208` | Command and Control (C2) server. |
| `ip_address_v4` | `172.86.76.127` | RouterHosting VPS used in the attack infrastructure. |
| `file_name` | `ms_upd.exe` | Malicious executable signed with a certificate previously used by MuddyWater. |
| `file_name` | `Game.exe` | Custom Remote Access Trojan. |

## Cyber Observables — Hunting Hints

Security teams may want to hunt for the following patterns to detect related activity:

| Type | Value | Description | Context |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `process_name` | `DWAService.exe` | Process associated with the DWAgent RMM tool. | Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR) logs, Process monitoring. |
| `process_name` | `AnyDesk.exe` | Process associated with the AnyDesk RMM tool, especially if installed outside of normal procedure. | EDR logs, Software inventory. |
| `network_traffic_pattern` | Outbound connections to `172.86.126.208` or `172.86.76.127` | Network traffic to known malicious infrastructure. | Firewall logs, Netflow, SIEM. |
| `command_line_pattern` | `powershell.exe -ExecutionPolicy Bypass` | Suspicious PowerShell execution, often used to load malicious scripts. | Windows Event ID 4688, EDR command line logging. |
| `log_source` | `Microsoft Teams Call/Meeting Logs` | Correlate suspicious endpoint activity with recent Teams sessions involving external parties. | Microsoft 365 audit logs. |

## Detection & Response

Detecting this campaign requires a multi-layered approach focusing on behavior rather than just signatures.

1.  **Endpoint Monitoring:** Use an EDR solution to monitor for the installation and execution of unauthorized remote access tools like `DWAgent` and `AnyDesk`. Create alerts for processes spawned by **[Microsoft Teams](https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/microsoft-teams/group-chat-software)**, especially command-line interpreters like `cmd.exe` or `powershell.exe`. D3FEND's [`D3-PA - Process Analysis`](https://d3fend.mitre.org/technique/d3f:ProcessAnalysis) is critical here.
2.  **Network Traffic Analysis:** Implement [`D3-NTA - Network Traffic Analysis`](https://d3fend.mitre.org/technique/d3f:NetworkTrafficAnalysis) to monitor for and alert on outbound connections to the known IOCs and other suspicious domains. Look for beaconing activity characteristic of RMM tools.
3.  **Log Auditing:** Ingest Microsoft 365 audit logs into a SIEM. Correlate security alerts on endpoints with Teams meeting logs to identify potential social engineering sessions. Monitor for suspicious file creation and script execution following interactive sessions with external users.
4.  **Incident Response:** If an infection is suspected, treat it as an APT incident, not a standard ransomware case. The goal is eviction, not just recovery. Isolate affected hosts, preserve forensic evidence, and conduct a thorough investigation to identify the full scope of the compromise, including lateral movement and data staging.

## Mitigation

Mitigation efforts should focus on user awareness, access controls, and hardening the environment.

1.  **User Training:** This is the most critical defense. Educate users about social engineering tactics, especially those involving interactive sessions and requests to run commands or enter credentials. Implement [`M1017 - User Training`](https://attack.mitre.org/mitigations/M1017/).
2.  **Application Control:** Use application allowlisting policies to prevent the execution of unauthorized software, including unapproved RMM tools. This aligns with [`M1038 - Execution Prevention`](https://attack.mitre.org/mitigations/M1038/).
3.  **MFA Hardening:** While the attack bypasses MFA through credential theft, continue to enforce it universally. This is a foundational control as per [`M1032 - Multi-factor Authentication`](https://attack.mitre.org/mitigations/M1032/). Augment with policies that require re-authentication for sensitive actions.
4.  **Network Segmentation:** Restrict outbound traffic from workstations to only what is necessary for business operations. This can help block C2 communications from unauthorized tools. This is a form of [`M1030 - Network Segmentation`](https://attack.mitre.org/mitigations/M1030/).
5.  **Endpoint Hardening:** Configure PowerShell to use Constrained Language Mode and enable script block logging to make malicious script execution more difficult and visible.
